sentences.text: Yes.
Yes, another escape from Brens to Lyon.
We just left-- like going out in the afternoon somewhere, and didn't come back.
We just took a train and left.
Well, we were under surveillance.
But it-- it was-- one was able to go out during the day with permission.
And we had to come back, of course.
But one was able to go out into that little town.
Mainly Jews, yes.
They were coming from-- lots of them were from Belgium, from Antwerp, from Brussels.
And then people that came to Antwerp and Brussels from Germany and Austria.
It was a mix, because when the war started, with the Maginot line, the Belgian Jews also fled.
I think it kept moving.
It kept moving.
Yes.
There was a camp council that decided on that, actually.
Yes.
But I don't remember exactly how it worked.
Get food.
[INAUDIBLE] Yeah.
No.
No.
It was mostly my mother who just went into different stores to try and bargain, I mean, barter for food.
That was the only reason she did that.
We stayed mainly within the camp.
Yes.
Not really, no.
There was just a lot of young people.
And we were mostly just singing together-- dancing, singing, dancing the Hora, things like that.
But it was a nice communal spirit, which helped us.
I know there also some Spaniards there still-- some Spaniards, and there were Zionists.
But I don't know if, at this point, if there was some resistance going on.
I don't know.
I wasn't aware of it.
Well, both my parents.
Yes.
Yes.
Hearing that we are going to go to another camp that was much more closed than Brens.
That people were going to be shipped out to a-- a-- a worse camp.
And they just didn't want to do that.
That's why.
I suppose so, yes.
It was still pretty possible there, whereas the other camps, like [INAUDIBLE], were totally cut off.
Once you were there, you were just ready to go into the wagons and being shipped by the Germans wherever they wanted to ship you.
But it's fantastic that my parents were aware of it, because many people were not.
A sixth sense.
I don't know.
Luck.
What can I say?
I consider it very, very lucky.
There was always information.
Through the grapevine, people heard things.
You know.
And they just told other people.
That's how it happened.
Not precisely, but I imagine that it was like in-- in the winter of 1940-- '41.
Well, what prompted the decision, after we lived in Lyon for about a year or so, it really became quite clear that France was going to be annexed.
The French-- the free part of France and the occupied part of France was going to be annexed.
And in that case, the Germans would be all over the place and we would have no way of saving ourselves.
So we left Lyon.
The nature of the community-- I-- I know that my sister went to school.
I worked.
And there were just-- the people of the same type of life just got together and-- and tried to help each other and do things.
There must have been a committee that one could go to and deal with, but I don't remember it.
Yeah, but there were also some Jews, some French Jews from Lyon that helped, like doctors and people like that.
I remember knowing a young doctor who took care of us, who examined us when we were sick and things like that.
That was the Lyon community-- the Lyon Jewish community.
Only we lived in a terrible house in a terrible part of Lyon.
It was bad because there were also French people living there from a very low denomination-- drunkards and people like that.
I remember the apartment next to us, there was a couple with small French children.
They were always drunk and the children cried all the time.
It was just an awful thing.
I had never witnessed anything like this.
But that's the only place we could find places to live.
No.
No, it wasn't a ghetto.
It was just the least nice places that one could-- the cheapest place that one could find a room or a small apartment.
I don't remember that, because I didn't-- I wasn't present at these things.
I just remember lots of other things, where everything was difficult and missing and bad.
Sometimes.
Sometimes, because we had my uncle and aunt in Lyon.
And we had some other family.
And we traveled with my aunt and uncle to Switzerland, to try to leave.
So we did see, as much as possible.
We got together and we helped each other.
No.
They just disappeared.
They took money and disappeared.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were French.
They were French peasants or something like that from that region.
Yeah.
I think it was a very gentle elderly man who was retired, and his wife and daughter.
They were very sweet.
They offered us food and shelter.
Otherwise, I don't know what he did in his life.
But they were nice, civilized people, you know?
Non-Jewish.
Non-Jewish definitely.
No.
It was just us and my aunt and uncle.
It was just six people.
We just stayed overnight.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We just stayed overnight and then left the next day at noon, when they told us it would be a good time to pass the border.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
I think they were great.
Well, they just told us that if we had gone at the time when we were supposed to go, we would have run right into the Germans.
And they were appalled that these French people would do that to us.
Obviously, the French people didn't care.
Yes.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Or it was totally careless, let's say.
Maybe it was just careless at this point.
Yes.
We came to Geneva, and it was September, 1942.
It was just around Rosh Hashanah.
And we stayed in a-- in a sports stadium, very primitively.
But the Jewish community of Geneva had arranged for a place to-- where we would be able to go to a synagogue and also where we would have meals served.
And it was just like coming right into paradise.
It was wonderful.
It was September.
The sun was shining.
We were eating real food.
We walked in the streets.
They had stores with chocolate.
And it was just like absolutely paradise.
I couldn't believe it.
There were movies.
It-- after-- since 1938, we didn't have this sensation of being in a real, wonderful place.
And then my father's brother, my uncle who was in the part of-- in the German-speaking part of Switzerland, who was a refugee in Switzerland, came immediately to greet us.
And he stayed with us a few days, and it was just indescribably wonderful.
They were just very warm, nice people.
And there were lots of refugees like us.
And we were very warmly-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were very nice to us.
And then after a few days, we were taken to a small village near Bern, and we were put into a school-- into a school house.
There were about, I would say, maybe 60 people, families, et cetera.
And that's where we stayed for a few months.
Eriswil.
No.
There were-- were no Jewish schools there.
We just-- no, we were actually-- I was not in school anymore.
Just people-- what always happened is that people between a whole group-- they're so mixed.
There were teachers.
People taught English.
Some people taught math.
We just did things between us.
People always did.
You could-- Some people taught you how to draw.
Whoever had a capacity to teach just did it.
That's all, without any formal way of doing it.
But we always learned something in there-- books.
It was wonderful in that sense, because wherever we went-- in different places, in different camps-- there were always people who gave of their knowledge.
Yes, absolutely.
That was all between us.
And everybody did something.
You cleaned.
Your washed.
You-- you-- you helped out.
You cooked.
You distributed the food.
You washed the dishes.
Everybody did something.
It was a real community.
Well, there was a little bit.
I mean, I-- I went out a couple of times to a-- I remember it was peasant's, and I was supposed to help them do things.
And it was wonderful because I helped them peel things and so on.
I did a little work.
And they gave us, at 4:00, they served us a-- a little [INAUDIBLE].
And it was great.
There was some real cream and butter and the best things.
It was so nice of them.
I loved to go there.
And they even lent me a pair of skis to learn how to ski.
And I was never very good in sports.
The first time I went down, I fell and the ski broke in four pieces.
And I never really tried it after that.
It's not my forte.
I'd rather read.
So-- but it was nice.
They tried to let us communicate with the Swiss people.
That was in September, right from Geneva, in September, 1942.
We stayed there a few months, and then they sent us to a camp near Zurich, which was called Adliswil.
And there, it was-- they had already arranged it.
We slept again in layers of bunks.
And we did all kind of work-- sewing, knitting.
There, I was still with my mother, because they had sent my father to another camp-- a working camp, somewhere in the Italian part of Switzerland, I think.
And we were separated at this point.
The Swiss-- the Swiss government.
The Swiss-- there was-- they had a-- every camp had two or three people.
One person all in charge, and then he had people working for him, who took care and who made all these decisions.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH].
There was a whole bunch of people who did this and this and this, who decided who does what, et cetera.
So they've sent my father to a real working camp.
They sent men to working camps, where they also did things outside like gardening and things like that.
Real work.
And some young people they took to build roads.
All kinds of work.
And a little bit later than that, we went to another camp, which then my parents were reunited.
It was a family camp.
And it wasn't really a camp.
It was a hotel.
It was a beautiful hotel in Lucerne, because the Swiss-- there was absolutely no tourist business.
And there, I really tried-- by that time I was 16, and I really wanted to do fashion, or art, something to do with art.
I was very eager to learn something.
So in Lucerne, they sent me to a place to learn how to do ceramics to start with.
So I learned how to do vases-- ceramics with strips of earth, and work them and turn them.
And it was quite nice.
But it was very cold in the winter.
And I got a lot of-- my hands and my feet got chilblains all over from that work with the very cold earth.
And then I-- I wrote to lots of people, to committees, et cetera, to be able to go to an art school.
And after-- but I didn't succeed.
Instead, they sent me to Bern, where a Jewish family said they would take care of me and send me to a school.
And then it turned out that they really just wanted to have a-- an in-servant, somebody who does things in the kitchen, et cetera.
I sta-- stayed there a few months and I was very unhappy, because they didn't send me to any school.
So I asked to go back to camp, which I was allowed.
Whereas my sister was sent to another family, a Swiss family, not Jewish.
And they were wonderful people and they taught her a lot of things.
And she went to school.
They sent her to school.
They didn't send me to school.
So I returned to camp, and I started writing for art schools.
At one point, I got a scholarship for Geneva.
And that was in 1945.
That was already at-- at the end of the war.
I got a scholarship to study art, and I was sent to Geneva and lived with a Swiss-Jewish family and went to art school every day-- a preparatory school.
Then there was vacation, where I was sent back to camp.
And for the next semester, I came back to Geneva.
And this is where I met my husband, [?
PPP. ?] He was also a refugee.
He was also from Vienna, surprisingly.
And he had gone through totally different experiences.
But we met there in art school in Geneva.
I don't remember the name of the camp.
I think it was [?
Davesco, ?] but I am not sure.
Yes.
He wanted to get back with us, and after a few months he succeeded.
He joined us in Lucerne, in the [INAUDIBLE], where everybody worked.
Everybody did something.
But it was fine.
Of that camp?
Oh yes.
Everybody, more or less the same way that we had.
Also people from all over-- Austria, Germany, Belgium, you name it.
Even-- there there were even French Jews, because at this point, they also escaped.
When France was annexed, lots of French Jews came also-- that had gone to the south of France from Paris and that were hidden.
And then they came to Switzerland.
I met a lot of French people there too.
At Tivoli?
Lots of them.
There must have been about 600 at least.
It was a big hotel.
It was being financed, I think, by lots of American committees, not really by the Swiss.
The Swiss got money from everybody, and they just tolerated us there for that reason.
I don't think it was Swiss money at all.
They're just kind of not very friendly.
Dour, I would say.
Very dour people.
There are some exceptions.
But not too friendly.
I suppose so, yes.
Because lots of them were like Germany and-- I mean, non-Jewish Swiss.
They didn't mind at all.
And lots of people were actually sent back, especially the ones that tried to cross the border directly from Austria and Germany, earlier.
Lots of them were sent back.
That my what?
Yes.
I think my mother was absolutely incredible, because she did most of everything.
My father, who used to be working and not being home, did some things.
But my mother is the woman-- the women that really held it all together.
Because she made sure that we had a warm clothes and a place to sleep and food.
I remember, even in Lyon, there was very little food.
We used to queue up in front of stores.
They said, today, this store has eggs.
So everybody stood for hours to get a couple of eggs.
Today, this store has sardines.
So my sister, my mother, and I would just stand in front of those stores for hours to get a box of sardines and to get this or that.
Then I remember there was a marvelous chocolate store in Lyon where they, every once in a while, sold marvelous chocolates.
Well, we queued up there and we bought the chocolates.
And my mother just sold them or exchanged them for food.
We never ate those marvelous chocolates because, for us, it was like money.
So I-- I will always remember there we get those wonderful chocolates and we couldn't eat them.
So all these things happened during Lyon.
You were in your late teens at this point.
Yeah.
No.
No.
I think the resistance was later.
The-- when we had left for Switzerland, that's when it became very, very strong.
When France-- free France was annexed.
No.
With the real conditions?
We only found out about it after the war, not before.
And it was just totally incredible.
Incredible, like a-- a figment of the imagination.
You couldn't believe that things like that could happen.
But things are happening now, like in Rwanda and everywhere, and if you're not there, you just can't grasp it.
It's something that you cannot grasp unless you have lived through it.
It's very difficult to grasp.
They might have.
They might have known earlier but not told us about it.
We knew it was dangerous, but not to the degree my parents knew.
And I don't think they knew what happened to my grandparents either, because there was absolutely no communication with Vienna until 1946, 1947.
One didn't know what happened to anybody.
Absolutely.
No.
It was a wonderful feeling.
You really felt that you were safe.
I mean, I felt it very, very strongly.
Yes, and we knew that Switzerland was neutral.
And we were hoping that it would stay neutral.
And somehow, it was a fantastic feeling of relief, of being back into a normal life.
When we were just, like, going from place to place to place and having less and less things, you know, and leaving always.
Whatever we had the last, we left there.
We left-- always had just a little package, a little suitcase.
That was it.
No.
We knew it was just a-- a phase of our life.
We didn't think we would stay there.
And then everybody was always hoping that the war would be over soon.
We didn't think it would take that long.
And we knew it was just part of it.
No.
They just-- they were so disgusted.
They were so unhappy about Vienna.
They found out about the family being deported and so on.
They never wanted to go back and live in Vienna.
They weren't sure where they wanted to live, but we had to leave Switzerland.
Switzerland didn't want to keep anybody for an extra minute if you could leave for anywhere.
So my parents just went back to the last place that they were, which was Lyon.
And I stayed because I was in school.
I wanted to finish the school.
Oh, well, it-- it was all over.
It was extraordinary in 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody knew.
I mean, it was very easy to know in Switzerland.
